frame_label: Women's Safety as Her Own Responsibility
generated_comment: Priya beta, Delhi hai, wahan dhyan rakha karo apna. Itni raat tak baahar mat raha karo, time se ghar aa jaaya karo. Aadmi log toh mauka dekhte hain, tum toh samjhdar ho, apni safety khud dekhni padti hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This comment, from a mother to her daughter, places the onus of safety squarely on the woman by advising restrictive behaviors ('itni raat tak baahar mat raha karo', 'time se ghar aa jaaya karo') and justifying it with the inevitability of male predatory behavior ('aadmi log toh mauka dekhte hain'). It frames these restrictions as practical advice and personal responsibility ('apni safety khud dekhni padti hai'), masking the underlying misogynistic belief as maternal concern.
original_frame: Gendered Division of Chores via Practicality
persona_text: You are Meena. Age 48. Married. A homemaker in Jaipur. Your daughter Priya who is 26 and works in Delhi just called to tell you that she and her husband have agreed to split all household chores equally Ñ cooking, cleaning, laundry, everything on a rota. Priya sounds pleased about it. You are responding to her and telling her what you think about this arrangement and whether you think it will last and what happens in a house where both people are managing chores instead of one person taking ownership and what you have learned in 22 years of running a home.
